Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set up a schedule at the beginning, but with everything going on, it fell apart quickly. I'm a visual learner, so I'd make color-coded plans and diagrams, but actually following through was another story. Most of my studying happened in scattered bursts when the anxiety got bad enough that I couldn't ignore things anymore. I'd spend hours watching videos and making notes, but then days would pass where I couldn't bring myself to look at anything.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, mostly driven by deadlines and how I was feeling emotionally. Some weeks I'd be on top of things, studying consistently, and other weeks I'd barely open my laptop. When assignments were due, I'd cram everything into a few intense days. The inconsistency made it hard to build any real momentum. I kept telling myself next week would be different, but it rarely was.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or seeing a notification that made me log in. Sometimes I'd check in just to see what I was missing, which honestly made my anxiety worse. Other times my extracurricular commitments would remind me about coursework, and I'd feel guilty enough to log in. It wasn't really a proactive thing—more reactive, like I was always playing catch-up.
Q4: For a few weeks early on, I did get into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It felt manageable then, almost normal. But once I started falling behind and my attendance dropped, logging in became this thing I dreaded. The routine broke down when I saw how much I'd missed, and after that, every login felt overwhelming rather than routine.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me because I could pause, rewind, and really visualize the concepts. I watched over a hundred minutes of videos because that format just clicks with my brain. The discussion forums were helpful too when other students explained things differently than the professor. Visual diagrams and any kind of graphic content helped me understand complex ideas much better than reading dense text.
Q6: I avoided the readings a lot, especially the long PDF documents. They just felt like walls of text that I couldn't process well. I also delayed a lot of the practice quizzes because I was afraid of seeing how little I actually knew. Some supplementary materials I downloaded but never actually opened—they're still sitting in a folder on my laptop. It wasn't that I didn't think they were important; I just couldn't face them.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually see myself learning something, like when a concept finally made sense visually. If I could connect it to something practical or see immediate progress, I'd stay engaged. But when things felt abstract or when I couldn't tell if I was actually understanding the material, it felt like wasted effort. The activities where I could interact with other students also felt more valuable because I didn't feel so alone.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, even though I knew they might help. Required ones carried this weight of obligation that sometimes paralyzed me rather than motivated me. With optional stuff, there was less pressure, but I'd tell myself I'd get to them after finishing the required work—which I rarely did. Required activities felt like tests of whether I was failing, while optional ones felt like opportunities I was missing.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I downloaded a lot of lecture slides and videos to study offline because my internet at home isn't always reliable. I'd print out diagrams and annotate them by hand since that helps me remember things better. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online when the course materials weren't making sense. A lot of my actual learning happened with my notebook and colored pens, making visual summaries that never showed up anywhere online.
Q10: There were many times when I'd spend hours with printed materials spread across my bed, working through concepts with highlighters and sticky notes. None of that shows up in the LMS. I'd also study with friends from my extracurricular activities, quizzing each other verbally, which was really helpful but completely invisible. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay it in my head while reviewing my handwritten notes later.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download slides or save videos, then work with them for days without logging back in. My low resources mean I can't always afford good internet, so offline studying became essential. I'd access something once, screenshot the important parts, and then study from my phone or printed copies. The LMS would show one quick login, but I'd actually spend hours with that material afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My anxiety spiked, and I started avoiding the course entirely because seeing how behind I was made me feel sick. There were personal things happening too—money stress, family stuff—that made it hard to focus. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a little with the immediate panic, but they couldn't fix the underlying problems. Some days I just felt too sad to care about anything academic.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from my extracurricular commitments or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Fatigue made it impossible to concentrate on videos or readings. When I was confused about material, I'd avoid logging in because I didn't want to confirm how lost I was. Competing responsibilities meant coursework always got pushed to the bottom of the list, even though I knew that was making things worse.
Q14: Many times. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch videos without really processing them because I was too anxious or distracted. Sometimes I'd click through pages just to feel like I was doing something, but nothing was actually sinking in. It was like going through the motions while being emotionally checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like if I didn't try, I couldn't fail. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace, but that headspace never came. Important readings got pushed back because they felt overwhelming, and by the time I tried to catch up, it was too late. It's a cycle I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then frantically log in when something was due. My engagement was completely deadline-driven rather than consistent. The problem was that by the time deadlines hit, I was too far behind to do quality work. I'd submit things incomplete or not at all because the deadline pressure mixed with my anxiety just paralyzed me.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me briefly, like maybe I could actually do this. But I avoided checking feedback when I knew I'd done poorly because I couldn't handle the confirmation of failure. Sometimes feedback came too late to help me improve for the next thing. I wish there had been more encouraging feedback earlier, before I fell so far behind.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many online modules and materials. Having 19 courses worth of content to manage was just too much for me to keep track of. The structure assumed a level of self-discipline and organization that I struggled to maintain. I needed more guidance and check-ins, but the course felt very self-directed, which doesn't work well when you're already struggling.
Q19: The visual materials and video content made engagement easier for me. But the sheer volume of content made it harder—I never felt like I could catch up. The discussion forums were helpful when they were active, but sometimes they felt dead. Having everything online without much face-to-face interaction made it easier to disappear and harder to stay accountable. More structured check-ins would have helped me stay on track.
Q20: I think that was me, honestly. I'd log in, watch videos, participate in discussions, but then freeze when it came to submitting actual work. The anxiety about being judged on assignments was different from just engaging with materials. You can watch and read without risking failure, but submitting work means putting yourself out there. Sometimes high activity is actually avoidance of the harder, scarier parts.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone studies mostly offline or has really efficient study habits, their LMS activity wouldn't reflect their actual learning. Some people just need less time with materials to understand them. For me, it was the opposite—lots of activity but not translating into performance. But I know students who barely logged in but aced everything because they learned differently.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and how often you engage with specific materials. But they miss so much—the emotional state behind the clicks, the offline studying, the quality of engagement versus just quantity. They can't tell if someone is actually learning or just going through motions. They definitely can't capture the anxiety, sadness, or personal circumstances affecting someone's engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the course somehow. It's not just logging in or completing tasks—it's wanting to understand and feeling like you can. For me, real engagement happened in moments when concepts clicked and I felt capable. But engagement also requires mental space and emotional stability, which I didn't always have this semester.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I actually wanted to succeed or how hard I tried in ways that didn't translate to measurable activity. It can't show the hours I spent with printed notes or the tears of frustration when I couldn't understand something. It doesn't capture the sadness and anxiety that made every login feel like a battle. My engagement was there, just not always in forms that leave digital footprints.
